name: Kalaris Therapeutics, Inc.
business: Kalaris Therapeutics is a clinical stage biopharmaceutical company dedicated to the development and commercialization of treatments for prevalent retinal diseases with major unmet medical needs. The company's lead product candidate, TH103, is a novel anti-VEGF drug engineered for extended intraocular retention and enhanced VEGF inhibition in patients with exudative and/or neovascular retinal diseases. TH103 is currently being evaluated in a Phase 1b/2 clinical trial for neovascular Age-related Macular Degeneration (nAMD).

exposures.sentence: We are developing TH103, a novel, clinical stage anti-vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) drug, specifically engineered to achieve extended intraocular retention with enhanced VEGF inhibition in patients with exudative and/or neovascular retinal diseases.

catalysts.event: Preliminary data from Phase 1b/2 trial of TH103 in nAMD
catalysts.sentence: We expect to share preliminary data from the ongoing Phase 1b/2 study in the first half of 2027.

catalysts.event: Initiation of Phase 3 clinical trials for TH103 in nAMD
catalysts.sentence: Assuming successful completion of the ongoing Phase 1b/2 clinical trial of TH103, and subject to favorable results from such trial and discussions with regulators, we intend to initiate Phase 3 clinical trials of TH103 for nAMD by year-end 2027.

tamClaims.claim: Global branded market for anti-VEGF retinal disease therapeutics was $15 billion in 2024.
tamClaims.sentence: Over the past 20 years, anti-VEGF therapeutics have revolutionized the treatment of prevalent exudative and/or neovascular retinal diseases, which represented an estimated $15 billion global branded market in 2024 based on publicly available SEC filings and publicly available regulatory documents reporting 2024 global net revenues for Eylea, Vabysmo, Lucentis and Eylea HD.

tamClaims.claim: An estimated 1.6 million adults in the United States have nAMD.
tamClaims.sentence: We are investigating TH103 as a treatment for patients with nAMD, a leading cause of blindness in the United States and Europe that affects an estimated 1.6 million adults in the United States.
